text: Hello, I am sharing a redo rectal prolapse case. This is a patient who had a resection rectopexy in an open procedure about twenty years ago. No mesh was used. And in the last couple of months has developed recurrent prolapse. And I selected this individual for a repeat pexy. And what you're seeing here is just the start of the mobilization to get the small bowel off of what I actually probably believe to be the old pexy site over the sacral promontory on the right. So just taking my time, sped up fast because it's not really that important for the surgery. But as we often have to do with redo cases, there are adhesions and you just gotta take your time and be safe so that you don't create further problems, which brings up the real reason for this video, because you'll identify at a certain point that a complication does occur and needs to be addressed. I want you to see if you can tell before you see the actual problem when and where the injury occurs.

The surgeon performs a repeat mobilization in the retro rectal plane, carefully navigating through scar tissue from a previous resection rectopexy. The presacral fascia is exposed, and the plane opens up smoothly. The ureter is identified during circumferential dissection, with attention to avoid injury to the bowel and adjacent structures. The surgeon works medially, noting scarring from prior surgery, and progresses towards identifying the distal anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels drawn up onto the left side of the colon conduit and carefully dissects them down to reach the medially dissected plane. The ureter is visible, and a sponge is used to maintain the surgical field. The surgeon continues the left-sided dissection down to the pelvic floor, encountering posterior adhesions likely from previous pexy sutures, which are easily separated. Despite scarring, the surgeon anticipates further dissection down to the floor and notes the absence of visible staples from the previous anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies a mesenteric hematoma, likely caused by a tear from the grasper, and applies pressure to stop the bleeding. The dissection continues into the rectovaginal septum, where the sigmoid or descending colon wall is directly adjacent to the vaginal wall, requiring careful manipulation due to the absence of mesorectal fat. The right side lateral stalks are preserved for pexy sutures.

The surgeon completes the left-sided dissection, palpating the anastomosis despite its lack of visual identification. The mesenteric hematoma appears to be resolved after holding pressure for ten minutes.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, noting some bleeding from the mesentery without pulsatile expansion. The dissection continues down to the pelvic floor, preparing for the pexy suture. Upon revisiting the site, the hematoma is observed to be expanding, prompting the surgeon to apply circumferential pressure using sponges for ten minutes to control the bleeding.

The surgeon applies focused pressure to a persistent mesenteric hematoma, checking its status after more than twenty minutes. The hematoma appears stable, allowing the surgeon to proceed with placing pexy sutures using 2-0 silk.

It looks horrific and obviously there's concern that you haven't controlled it and that you'll leave the operating room and the patient will continue to bleed and end up with a serious problem or that if you're too aggressive in your attempts at hemostasis with sutures or cautery or digging into the hematoma, that you can end up making this bowel ischemic. And if that were the case, I would have to do a redo resection, and then I would have to go down below her previous anastomosis, and that would end up being a coloanal. If it needs to be done, it needs to be done. But obviously, I just wanted to try to control the bleeding. But after doing those stitches, everything kind of looks okay to me. There's no more expansion. There's no more bleeding. But now I'm seeing on the left lateral aspect of things, maybe there's a development. I can't tell if it's just dissected around to the other side. I check perfusion in the area where the hematoma is obscuring the bowel wall. perfusion seems fine. So at this point, I'm feeling good.

The surgeon observes active bleeding from the bowel and attempts focused cautery, which is ineffective. Holding a clamp stops the bleeding temporarily, but it resumes upon release. Hemostatic agents, gel foam, and thrombin are applied to the bleeding site. A rigid sigmoidoscopy reveals pink mucosa with no mucosal bleeding. The surgeon removes the hemostatic agents, but bleeding persists, prompting the placement of a focused suture directly at the bleeding site to control it.

The surgeon inspects the mesentery for bleeding, noting a reduction in bleeding intensity. Using 3-0 silk sutures, the surgeon places mattress sutures above and below the bleeding site. A sheet of Surgicel is then laid beneath these sutures to aid in hemostasis.

The surgeon ties down sutures to apply pressure and achieve tamponade, checking for bleeding on the left side of the bowel. After waiting for more than ten minutes, perfusion is assessed, and the bowel appears healthy. A rigid sigmoidoscopy confirms no mucosal bleeding, and the procedure is terminated with the patient observed overnight.
